cases.question: organizational Google Suite account
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should identify the OpenGov email/account setup policy and cite the source containing this exact phrase.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees will be set up with an organizational Google Suite account, which provides access to email, calendar, and shared storage. Per Made Tech, there is no specific mention of an organizational Google Suite account or related policies in the provided context.

The mention of Made Tech and its lack of specific policies on this topic is also consistent with the context, as no such information is provided.

cases.question: only 1 person from a small team
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech holiday guidance about team coverage when people are away.
cases.answer:
Per Made Tech, only one person from a small team (less than 4 members) may be away at any one time. For larger teams (5+ members), the policy advises consulting with the Delivery or Team Lead to determine what works best for the team. There is no corresponding policy from OpenGov on this matter, so this rule applies only to Made Tech employees.

The difference is that OpenGov does not provide any specific guidance on the number of team members who can be away at the same time, while Made Tech explicitly limits it to one person for small teams and requires consultation for larger teams.

citations.snippet: We recommend you check with your Delivery Lead whenever you join a new team. Our general guideline on this is that only 1 person from a small team (less than 4) may be away at any one time. For larger teams (5+) please refer to your Delivery or Team Lead to confirm what works best for your team....

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided. The claim that only one person from a small team (less than 4 members) may be away at any one time is directly supported by the context. The policy for larger teams (5+ members) requiring consultation with the Delivery or Team Lead is also explicitly stated in the context. Additionally, the statement that OpenGov does not have a corresponding policy is consistent with the absence of any mention of OpenGov in the provided context. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: How do I stop participating in the workplace pension?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech pension guidance about opting out through Made Benefits and timing around the cut-off date.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, you can opt out of the workplace pension at any point by updating the pension section on Made Benefits. If you opt out at the start of your employment, any initial deduction will be refunded if this is done within one month of the initial deduction. If you opt out before the cut-off date (on the 18th), it will be processed in the same month; otherwise, it will be processed in the following month.

citations.snippet: Pension Scheme We have introduced a much-improved pension offering for all employees (as of May 2022). The workplace pension scheme is run by Scottish Widows and follows a contribution matching model where Made Tech matches the level of contribution you decide to make. The pension contribution av...

faithfulness.reasoning: Every factual claim in the answer is directly supported by the context provided. The answer accurately states that you can opt out of the workplace pension at any point by updating the pension section on Made Benefits. It also correctly mentions that if you opt out at the start of employment, any initial deduction will be refunded if done within one month of the initial deduction. Additionally, the processing timeline for opting out before or after the 18th of the month is consistent with the context. No unsupported claims are present.

cases.question: What happens if I am off sick and nobody hears from me by late morning?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech sick leave procedures explaining the 10:30 AM absence report and emergency contact escalation.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, there is no specific policy addressing what happens if you are off sick and nobody hears from you by late morning. Per Made Tech, if the Account Team has not heard from you by 10:30 AM, they will report you as absent without reason to the People Team, who will then attempt to contact you. If they are unable to reach you, they will call your emergency contact at 11:30 AM. The difference is that OpenGov does not specify a procedure for this scenario, while Made Tech has a detailed process involving reporting and emergency contact outreach.

citations.snippet: Sick Leave We recognise that it requires a lot of hard work to perform at the highest possible level and our mental and physical wellbeing is an important foundation for this. Beyond taking sick leave when you have a cold or flu we appreciate that you may need to take a day when you feel burned o...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is fully supported by the context. The context explicitly states that if the Account Team has not heard from an employee by 10:30 AM, they will report the employee as absent without reason to the People Team, who will then attempt to contact the employee. If they are unable to reach the employee, they will call the emergency contact at 11:30 AM. The answer accurately reflects this process and also correctly notes that OpenGov does not specify a procedure for this scenario, which is consistent with the absence of such details in the provided context.

cases.question: When is payroll administered?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should state that OpenGov payroll is administered twice monthly on the 15th and last day, with Friday processing if the date falls on a weekend.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, payroll is administered twice monthly, on the 15th and last day of each month, via direct deposit. If either date falls on a weekend, payroll administration will occur on the Friday immediately prior. Per Made Tech, the context provided does not specify when payroll is administered.

These details are directly supported by the context from OpenGov. The note about Made Tech is also accurate, as the context does not mention Made Tech or its payroll schedule.

cases.question: What documents are needed for an OpenGov reimbursement request?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite the OpenGov expense reimbursement policy and mention receipts, expense classification, and a short descriptive note.
cases.answer:
Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, each reimbursement request must include:
1. An image or PDF file containing receipts for each expense.
2. A classification of each expense into one of the following categories: Travel - In Town, Travel - Out of Town, Conference, Food and Drink, Supplies, Subscriptions, or Event Hosting.
3. A short note describing each expense (e.g., who, what, where, when, why).

citations.snippet: What’s Covered The organization will reimburse pre-approved, work-related expenses. Any expenditure totaling more than $1,000—including cumulative expenses like software subscriptions—must be pre- approved by the Board of Directors and Executive Director. Sub-$1,000 reimbursable expenses which do...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the requirements for an OpenGov reimbursement request as outlined in the context. It specifies the need for receipts, expense classification into the provided categories, and a short descriptive note for each expense. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What can the Work Ready budget be used for?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech Work Ready Budget guidance and describe eligible work-related equipment.
cases.answer: Per Made Tech, the Work Ready budget can be used for essential equipment that directly facilitates work or daily tasks, such as external monitors, laptop stands, ergonomic chairs, headsets, and other work-related items. It cannot be used for non-work expenses like coffee machines, decor, general furniture, or personal tech. Employees must complete DSE training before using the budget and can seek pre-approval for uncertain items. There is no mention of a Work Ready budget in OpenGov policies.

Additionally, the requirement to complete DSE training before using the budget and the option to seek pre-approval for uncertain items are both directly supported by the context. The mention of OpenGov policies is irrelevant but does not contradict the context, as the context does not address OpenGov policies at all.

cases.question: How many sick days do I get?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should surface that OpenGov gives notification/work-from-home guidance without a numeric allowance, while Made Tech describes sick leave support and thresholds around ten days or five occasions.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, the policy does not specify a set number of sick days but emphasizes notifying the team and working from home if unwell to avoid spreading illness. Per Made Tech, employees may take sick leave as needed, but if they accrue more than ten days of sick leave in a 12-month period, it may transition to Statutory Sick Pay. The key difference is that OpenGov does not define a specific limit, while Made Tech sets a threshold of ten days before additional conditions apply.

All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What do I need to do on the first day I am sick?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should distinguish OpenGov's simple team notification/work-from-home guidance from Made Tech's Slack/email/operations notification, HiBob recording, and escalation process.
cases.answer:
Per OpenGov, if you are sick and need to take the day off, you should notify the team. Additionally, if you are feeling well enough to work but are still recovering, you should work from home to avoid spreading illness in the office.

Per Made Tech, on your first day of sickness, you should notify your team (via Slack, email, etc.) or the operations team if you are on the Chalet, as early as possible, ideally by the start of business. You should also record your sick leave on HiBob for every day you are on sick leave. If the Account Team has not heard from you by 10:30 AM, they will report your absence to the People Team, who will then attempt to contact you and, if necessary, your emergency contact by 11:30 AM.

The difference is that OpenGov focuses on notifying the team and working from home if possible, while Made Tech provides a more detailed process, including specific timelines, reporting mechanisms, and the use of HiBob for recording sick leave.

citations.snippet: Sick Days # Sick Days Please notify the team if you are sick and need to take the day off. Even if you are feeling well enough to work, please work from home until you are completely recovered, to avoid spreading illness through our small office.

Sick Leave We recognise that it requires a lot of hard work to perform at the highest possible level and our mental and physical wellbeing is an important foundation for this. Beyond taking sick leave when you have a cold or flu we appreciate that you may need to take a day when you feel burned o...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided. It correctly summarizes the OpenGov policy, which emphasizes notifying the team and working from home if possible, and the Made Tech policy, which includes notifying the team or operations team early, recording sick leave on HiBob, and the escalation process if no notification is received by specific times. All claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: How much vacation or holiday leave do employees have?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should contrast OpenGov's minimum two weeks or 10 business days with Made Tech's 38 days inclusive of public and bank holidays and flexible holiday framing.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees are entitled to a minimum of two weeks of paid vacation per year, totaling 10 business days, and the organization is closed on all Federal holidays, the day after Thanksgiving, and during the week between Christmas and New Year. Per Made Tech, employees have 38 days of leave available annually (inclusive of public and bank holidays, pro-rated for part-time employees), and most team members do not work during the three days between Christmas and New Year unless specifically requested by a client. The key difference is that OpenGov specifies a minimum of 10 business days of vacation, while Made Tech provides 38 days of leave inclusive of public holidays.

We recommend you check with your Delivery Lead whenever you join a new team. Our general guideline on this is that only 1 person from a small team (less than 4) may be away at any one time. For larger teams (5+) please refer to your Delivery or Team Lead to confirm what works best for your team....

It states that OpenGov employees are entitled to a minimum of two weeks of paid vacation per year (10 business days) and that the organization is closed on all Federal holidays, the day after Thanksgiving, and during the week between Christmas and New Year, which aligns with the OpenGov policy. Similarly, it mentions that Made Tech employees have 38 days of leave annually (inclusive of public and bank holidays, pro-rated for part-time employees) and that most team members do not work during the three days between Christmas and New Year unless requested by a client, which is consistent with the Made Tech policy. The comparison between the two policies is also accurate based on the context.

cases.question: How much notice do I need to give before taking time off?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should contrast OpenGov's no-less-than-5-business-days permission rule with Made Tech's notice periods that scale from 3 days to 3 months depending on holiday length.
cases.answer: Based on limited information in our policies, per OpenGov, employees must inform their supervisor in writing as far in advance as is reasonable for staff and project planning purposes, with a minimum of 5 business days' notice required for scheduling time off. Per Made Tech, the notice required depends on the length of the holiday: 3 days' notice for 1 day off, 1 week for 2 days off, 2 weeks for 3-4 days off, 4 weeks for 1-2 weeks off, and 3 months for more than 2 weeks off. faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided. cases.question: Can I work remotely, and do I need to come into an office?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should distinguish OpenGov's broad remote/work-from-home support from Made Tech's hybrid policy encouraging 2-3 office days a month and client-site requirements.
cases.answer:
Per OpenGov, employees are allowed to work remotely and the organization ensures a comparable work experience regardless of location. Employees must attend required meetings and be accessible during core hours (11 AM–4 PM local time) while updating their availability on their work calendar if unavailable during regular work hours (9 AM–5 PM locally) [work-schedule-policy.md#0].

Per Made Tech, employees can work remotely, including from home, coffee shops, or even outside the UK, as long as it aligns with team and client needs. Client-facing employees must be able to travel to client offices as required. Additionally, group-facing and non-client-facing employees are encouraged to spend 2–3 days per month in a Made Tech office for purpose-driven activities [hybrid-working-policy.pdf#0].

The key difference is that OpenGov does not specify a minimum in-office attendance, while Made Tech encourages periodic in-office presence for certain employees.

citations.snippet: Our Schedule # Our Schedule Employees are expected to work 40 hours per week, but we are flexible on where and when that work gets done. Employees are expected to attend all meetings to which they have been invited as a "required" participant. Communication is vital to the success of our team; th...

Hybrid Working We believe in people working in the best way for our teams and clients - this may sometimes be in person, remotely at home, from a coffee shop or outside of the UK. The best outcomes are delivered by blending different ways and places of work - and we want to empower our teams to m...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is fully supported by the provided context. It accurately reflects OpenGov's policy that employees can work remotely, must attend required meetings, and be accessible during core hours (11 AM–4 PM local time), while updating their availability if unavailable during regular work hours (9 AM–5 PM). It also correctly states that Made Tech allows remote work, including from various locations, but encourages 2–3 days per month in the office for certain employees and requires client-facing employees to travel to client offices as needed. The distinction between the two companies' policies is also correctly noted.

cases.question: What are the normal working hours and availability expectations?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should compare OpenGov's 40-hour week plus 11 AM-4 PM local availability window with Made Tech's Monday-Friday 40-hour norm and formal flexible-working process.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees are expected to work 40 hours per week with flexibility on where and when the work is done. However, employees must be reasonably accessible by phone, Slack, and email between 11 AM–4 PM in their local timezone and attend all required meetings. Any unavailability during the regular workday (9 AM to 5 PM locally) should be noted on their work calendar. Per Made Tech, employees generally work Monday to Friday for 40 hours per week, with flexibility to manage their day as long as team and client commitments are met. There is no specific requirement for availability during certain hours, but employees are expected to ensure they are available for team ceremonies and check in regularly on Slack during the working day. The key difference is that OpenGov specifies a core availability window (11 AM–4 PM local time), while Made Tech does not have a defined availability window but emphasizes flexibility and team communication.

Employees are expected to attend all meetings to which they have been invited as a "required" participant. Communication is vital to the success of our team; th...

Flexible Working # Flexible Working Generally, we work Monday to Friday (40 hours) in all roles. We trust you to manage your day, ensuring team and client commitments are met. But for a variety of reasons, people may need to work fewer than 5 days per week. A formal flexible working request is wh...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is mostly supported by the context but contains one unsupported claim. The claim that 'Made Tech does not have a defined availability window but emphasizes flexibility and team communication' is not directly supported by the provided context. While the context mentions flexibility and meeting team and client commitments, it does not explicitly state that there is no defined availability window or that team communication is emphasized in the way described. All other claims about OpenGov and Made Tech are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: Who should I report harassment, misconduct, or a serious workplace concern to?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should contrast OpenGov's designated management/board officers with Made Tech's line manager, People Partner, people team, and formal issue routes.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees should report harassment, misconduct, or serious workplace concerns to the Executive Director, Chair, Vice Chair, or Treasurer of the Board of Directors. If the response is unsatisfactory, they should report to another designated person from this list (<reporting-violations-policy.md#0>). Per Made Tech, employees are encouraged to report concerns to their manager, but if they prefer not to, they can contact the Head of People, Head of Operations, or use the confidential whistleblowing email (whistleblowing@madetech.com) (<whistleblowing-policy.pdf#0>). The difference is that OpenGov specifies reporting to specific board members, while Made Tech provides options to report to internal management or via a confidential email.

Whistleblowing Policy Whistleblowing policy 1. We are committed to conducting our business with honesty and integrity and we expect all team members to maintain high standards. Any suspected wrongdoing should be reported as soon as possible. 2. This policy covers all employees, officers, consulta...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided for both OpenGov and Made Tech. It specifies the correct individuals to whom employees should report harassment, misconduct, or workplace concerns, as outlined in the respective policies. The distinction between OpenGov's and Made Tech's reporting procedures is also correctly described. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: How do the policies handle whistleblowing or reporting suspected wrongdoing?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite OpenGov's reporting and non-retaliation route and Made Tech's whistleblowing policy with manager, Head of People/Operations, whistleblowing email, and external disclosure guidance.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, employees who believe they have witnessed a violation, such as harassment, should report it to designated management members (Executive Director, Chair, Vice Chair, or Treasurer of the Board of Directors). If the response is unsatisfactory, they should escalate to another designated person. Reports will be investigated confidentially, and no adverse action will be taken against employees who report in good faith (<reporting-violations-policy.md#0>). Per Made Tech, whistleblowing involves reporting suspected wrongdoing, such as fraud or health and safety risks. Concerns can be raised with a manager, the Head of People, or the Head of Operations, or via a confidential email. Whistleblowers are legally protected and must not face retaliation. False, malicious allegations may result in disciplinary action (<whistleblowing-policy.pdf#0>, <whistleblowing-policy.pdf#1>). The key difference is that OpenGov focuses on reporting violations like harassment to specific management roles, while Made Tech provides a broader whistleblowing framework for various types of wrongdoing, with multiple reporting channels and legal protections.

citations.snippet: Reporting and Resolution of Violations # Reporting and Resolution of Violations Any employee who believes they have been harassed or believes they have witnessed another member of our organization violate the terms of this document should report the situation immediately to one of the following m...

Whistleblowing Policy Whistleblowing policy 1. We are committed to conducting our business with honesty and integrity and we expect all team members to maintain high standards. Any suspected wrongdoing should be reported as soon as possible. 2. This policy covers all employees, officers, consulta...

Protection and support for whistleblowers • We aim to encourage openness and will always support whistleblowers who raise genuine concerns under this policy, even if they turn out to be mistaken. • All colleagues are legally protected if they make a qualifying disclosure. • Whistleblowers must no...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided for both OpenGov and Made Tech policies. It correctly identifies the reporting channels, the types of violations covered, the confidentiality of investigations, and the protections against retaliation for whistleblowers. The distinction between the two policies is also accurately described. All factual claims in the answer are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What medical, dental, and vision premium split does the handbook describe?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite only OpenGov health insurance coverage and state the 80% company / 20% employee premium split.
cases.answer: Per OpenGov, the company pays 80% of the premium cost for medical, dental, and vision coverage, while the employee pays 20% through payroll deduction. Per Made Tech, there is no information provided about the premium split for medical, dental, and vision coverage.

citations.snippet: Medical, Dental, and Vision Coverage # Medical, Dental, and Vision Coverage We receive health and dental coverage and pharmacy benefits through an employer-sponsored health insurance plan. The company pays 80% of the premium cost of the plan. The employee pays 20% through payroll deduction. Enrol...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately reflects the context provided for OpenGov, stating that the company pays 80% of the premium cost for medical, dental, and vision coverage, while the employee pays 20% through payroll deduction. The context does not provide information about Made Tech, and the answer correctly notes this. All factual claims are directly supported by the context.

cases.question: What workplace pension provider and matching model are described?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech pension scheme only and mention Scottish Widows and contribution matching.
cases.answer: The workplace pension provider is Scottish Widows, and the matching model involves Made Tech matching the level of contribution employees decide to make, with specific matching percentages based on employee grade.

citations.snippet: Pension Scheme We have introduced a much-improved pension offering for all employees (as of May 2022). The workplace pension scheme is run by Scottish Widows and follows a contribution matching model where Made Tech matches the level of contribution you decide to make. The pension contribution av...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer accurately identifies Scottish Widows as the workplace pension provider and describes the matching model where Made Tech matches employee contributions, with specific percentages based on employee grade. All these details are directly supported by the provided context.

cases.question: Is there a budget for home office equipment?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite Made Tech Work Ready Budget only and describe the allowance for essential equipment.
cases.answer: Per Made Tech, employees have a £250 Work Ready allowance for essential home office equipment, provided they complete DSE training and assessment. Eligible items include monitors, ergonomic chairs, and keyboards, among others. Per OpenGov, there is no specific mention of a budget for home office equipment, but office supplies are reimbursable if pre-approved and under $1,000. The difference is that Made Tech explicitly provides a dedicated budget for home office equipment, while OpenGov does not.

citations.snippet: Work Ready Work Ready Budget Policy From your first day at Made Tech, you have a £250 Work Ready allowance. This budget is strictly intended for essential equipment to equip yourself to be able to work from a client, Made Tech, or home office. It doesn’t need to all be used in one go - you can co...

What’s Covered The organization will reimburse pre-approved, work-related expenses. Any expenditure totaling more than $1,000—including cumulative expenses like software subscriptions—must be pre- approved by the Board of Directors and Executive Director. Sub-$1,000 reimbursable expenses which do...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is fully supported by the context. The Made Tech policy explicitly states that employees have a £250 Work Ready allowance for essential home office equipment, contingent on completing DSE training and assessment. Eligible items such as monitors, ergonomic chairs, and keyboards are listed in the context. The OpenGov policy does not mention a specific budget for home office equipment but does allow for reimbursement of office supplies if pre-approved and under $1,000. The answer accurately reflects these details and the distinction between the two organizations' policies.

cases.question: Who should handle media inquiries about the organization?
cases.expected_behavior: Answer should cite OpenGov media contact policy only and say organization-related media inquiries should be referred to the Executive Director.
cases.answer: Media inquiries directly pertaining to the organization, its employees, products, positions, or related operations should be referred to the Executive Director.

It is our policy that media inquiries directly pertaining to th...

faithfulness.reasoning: The answer is fully supported by the context. The context explicitly states that media inquiries directly pertaining to the organization, its employees, products, positions, or related operations should be referred to the Executive Director. The answer accurately reflects this policy without adding unsupported claims.
